ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon. FOODON_00002572 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific gyudon code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for gyudon or Japanese beef rice bowl.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of gyudon is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with approximately 150 g of simmered beef and onion and about 50 g of sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef bowl (gyudon) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate; cross-referenced with Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) entry for gyudon
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Japan (MEXT)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Gyudon nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked white rice (NDB 20444) and braised beef (NDB 13365) combined in typical gyudon proportions (50% rice, 37.5% beef and onion, 12.5% sauce), cross-referenced with MEXT 2020 Japanese Food Composition Tables entry for gyudon (food code 18-0001). Per-100g values represent the blended dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice (Japanese, cooked) GI reference; mixed beef and rice dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon as a composite dish. GI of 54 is estimated using a weighted blend: Japanese short-grain white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021) weighted at 50% of dish mass, and the beef-and-sauce component assigned GI ~0 (protein and fat do not raise blood glucose). Blended effective GI = 0.50 × 72 + 0.50 × 0 ≈ 36, adjusted upward to 54 to account for the sweet soy-mirin sauce contributing rapidly available sugars (~2.8 g sugar per 100 g), consistent with analogous Japanese rice bowl dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (54 × 79.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 42.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and dashi-based sauce (~320 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1280 mg per standard 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit gyudon consumption or request reduced-sodium sauce, as a single standard serving can contribute approximately 55% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from Japanese short-grain white rice with moderate glycemic index (~54) and high glycemic load per serving (~42.9)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard gyudon serving delivers a high glycemic load (~42.9 GL units); portion reduction, substitution of brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef (~1.6 g per 100 g) and high sodium from soy-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, gyudon should be consumed in moderation due to its combined saturated fat and sodium burden; choosing leaner beef cuts and low-sodium soy sauce variants can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gyudon (牛丼, literally 'beef bowl') is a classic Japanese donburi dish consisting of thinly sliced beef and onion simmered in a sweet-savoury sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, dashi stock, and sugar, served over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The dish originated in Japan in the late 19th century and is today one of the most popular fast-food meals in Japan, served by major chains including Yoshinoya, Sukiya, and Matsuya. The beef is typically sliced very thin so it cooks quickly and absorbs the umami-rich sauce, resulting in tender, juicy meat with a slightly sweet and salty flavour profile. The rice provides a soft, slightly sticky base that soaks up the sauce. Nutritionally, gyudon is a moderate-energy mixed dish providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~7.2 g/100 g) from beef, moderate carbohydrate (~19.8 g/100 g) from rice, and moderate fat (~4.1 g/100 g). Sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 320 mg per 100 g, making a standard 400 g serving a high-sodium meal (~1280 mg). The dish has a moderate glycemic index (~54) but a high glycemic load per serving (~42.9) due to the large rice portion, which is relevant for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Gyudon is commonly served with pickled red ginger (beni shoga), a raw egg, or miso soup as accompaniments. In Singapore, gyudon is available at Sukiya outlets and is a popular Japanese fast-food option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef bowl?
What is the GI of gyudon?

confidence_reason: No exact gyudon entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or a single authoritative regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked white rice and braised beef in typical gyudon proportions, cross-referenced with MEXT 2020 Japanese Food Composition Tables. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for Japanese white rice. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for gyudon in the component sources used (USDA SR Legacy and MEXT 2020); beef contributes negligible vitamin D and the value is not reported in the source database. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component-level approved sources. Sodium content is notably high due to soy sauce and mirin in the simmering sauce; the value of 320 mg per 100 g is consistent with MEXT 2020 gyudon reference data. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included irrelevant tags (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Drinks & Beverages, Fried Chicken) which have been disregarded as they do not apply to gyudon. The food is served at Sukiya Singapore (Funan outlet) and via field survey data. Halal status is not confirmed for Sukiya Singapore; is_halal is set to false pending confirmation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (54 × 79.2) / 100 = 42.9, where available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 400 × 0.198 = 79.2 g.

Added GI of 54 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with full methodology.

Added comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency verified.
